text: The surgical team prepares for a robotic retroperitoneal lymph node dissection on a 33-year-old male patient with a residual mass in the left renal hilum. The patient is positioned supine with arms tucked and legs straight, in a 20-degree Trendelenburg position. The surgical setup includes a four-port configuration with one assistant port, positioned below the umbilicus and projecting upwards towards the patient's head. The camera, equipped with a 30-degree lens, is positioned in the right lower quadrant, alongside robotic arms equipped with hot shears and a ProGrasp instrument.

The surgeon begins by creating an incision from the cecum to the ligament of Treitz, cutting through the posterior peritoneum. The cut edges of the posterior peritoneum are sutured to the abdominal wall to maintain exposure and keep the small bowel out of the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the right iliac artery, ureter, and appendix, then makes an incision underneath the cecum through the posterior peritoneum. A flap is created and suspended to enhance exposure of the surgical field.

The surgeon uses bipolar forceps for blunt dissection, preferring them over the sharper Maryland dissector. A flap is being created with deliberate thickness, revealing the bowel and bowel mesentery. A lymph node is visible within the mesentery, but it is not the target of concern.

The surgeon moves to the left side, identifying a cut edge and postganglionic nerve fibers from the hypogastric plexus. The left common iliac artery is visible, and the lateral limit is determined to be the ureter. The surgeon examines a structure that may be the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA).
The surgeon uses two needle drivers to position a long suspension suture, a 3-0 monofilament suture with an SH needle, to elevate the right cut edge of the posterior peritoneum.

The surgeon adjusts the position to optimize exposure, ensuring the small bowel remains stable and does not fall into the surgical field. A Hemolock is used to secure structures, and the cecum is left intact to prevent the small bowel from shifting underneath.

The surgeon continues the dissection cephalad, identifying the duodenum and a blue structure, likely the left renal vein or possibly the inferior vena cava. The surgeon prepares to take the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) for better exposure due to a left-sided tumor, using scissors and bipolar forceps.

The surgeon identifies and defines anatomical structures including the left renal vein, ureter, and the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) beneath the left mesocolon. Large lymphatic channels originating from the pancreas are visible, crossing over the left renal vein, and are prepared for clipping.

The surgeon uses a robotic clip applier to secure a large lymphatic channel over the left renal vein, preventing potential chylous ascites. Multiple clips are applied to ensure closure of the lymphatic vessels, with adjustments made for optimal exposure and angle.

The surgeon identifies and manipulates lymphatic structures beneath the aorta, noting the presence of the gonadal vein and lumbar vein. The renal artery is visible, and needle drivers are prepared for suturing.

The surgeon carefully dissects lymphatic tissue from a large artery, preparing to clip it. Hemolox clips are applied to secure the artery, and the dissection proceeds from the bottom upwards to reveal the surgical space. The inferior mesenteric artery is visible, and the surgeon notes the presence of good collateral flow to the colon in young patients.
The surgeon identifies the general femoral nerve and begins dissecting the cord on the left side of the aorta, focusing on this area for the majority of the procedure.
The surgeon identifies a lymph node and applies a clip. The cord is seen crossing the ureter, and there is a small amount of tissue, possibly perinephric fat or an artery, which is also addressed.

The surgeon uses the DaVinci XI robotic system to manipulate the arms for enhanced backward motion, allowing access to the pelvic region. The vessel sealer is prepared to take the lumbar arteries and veins, while the ureter crossing the iliac marks the lower boundary of the node dissection. Lymphatic tissue is being removed, and the surgeon navigates the robotic arms to reach the inguinal canal without re-docking.

The surgeon pushes the iliac down, revealing the common iliac vein and a small branch. Clips are applied to the distal extent of the para-aortic nodes. The sympathetic trunk is identified as a white structure near the general femoral nerve, and the surgeon prepares to follow it upwards.

The surgeon identifies and examines the postganglionic fibers of the lumbar sympathetic trunk on the left side, noting their small size. The hypogastric plexus is visible, with nerve fibers located between the inferior vena cava and the aorta.
The surgeon clips the left-sided fibers, using a sharper instrument to ensure precision in the dissection.
The surgeon identifies a large postganglionic fiber coming off the sympathetic chain and prepares to cut it. The fiber is visible within the surgical field, surrounded by lymphatic tissue.

The surgeon identifies an open channel potentially causing a chylous leak and applies a robotic clip to prevent leakage. The surgical field is cleaned using suction to assess the effectiveness of the clip placement.

The surgeon uses robotic clips to divide lymphatic channels near the interaortic cable to prevent spillage, separating para-aortic, interaortic, and pre-aortic regions. The right and left renal arteries are visible, and the surgeon prepares to use a vessel sealer on the lumbar region.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer in the left hand to address the anterior spinous ligament, while identifying and clipping a cluster of lymphatics near the lumbar region. The gonadal vein and artery are exposed, ensuring the ureter is safely distant from the surgical field. Suction is applied to clear fluid accumulation around the lymphatic channels.

The surgeon works on the lateral extent of the surgical field, identifying and freeing the artery and the cord from surrounding tissue. Suction is applied to clear the area, revealing large channels and the beginning of the cruciate diaphragm.

The surgeon identifies a large vein and clips it to ensure hemostasis, while holding the aorta back for better visibility. Additional clips are applied to prevent lymphatic leakage, and the dissection is assessed for effectiveness.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to address bleeding, then proceeds with nerve-sparing dissection by splitting over the cava, separating the interaortic cable from the paracable. The cord structure is visible on one side, and the surgeon focuses on the cable side, identifying a small fiber and preparing to address a larger one.

The surgeon identifies small lumbar fibers and post-ganglionic trunks, using cold dissection to carefully separate them from surrounding lymphatics. Three trunks are visible, which are traced towards the hypogastric plexus located atop the lymphatic tissue.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the hypogastric plexus, cutting a large nerve trunk on the left side while sparing the right side. The retro-aortic area is addressed, ensuring removal of lymphatic tissue from the region.

The surgeon focuses on dissecting and preserving a large nerve trunk, while identifying and managing smaller lumbar nerves. Clips are applied to secure structures, potentially including the cisterna chyli, as the team works to maintain nerve integrity and avoid damage.
The surgeon identifies the cisterna chyli and prepares to clip it, noting its large size and resemblance to bowel tissue. The assistant uses suction on both sides of the structure to manage fluid.

The surgeon uses scissors to expose a large lymphatic channel, which is then clipped using a surgical clip. A grasper is employed to manipulate the tissue around the inter-aortic cable area, focusing on nerve sparing techniques. The sigmoid colon is visible but not involved in the current dissection.

The surgeon identifies and leaves the nerves intact while addressing a consistent vein prone to bleeding. The para cable lymph nodes on the right side are being dissected, following the completion of the inter-to-cable dissection with nerve sparing. The lymph nodes are taken down to the point where the ureter crosses the right common iliac artery, and the distal extent is clipped. The procedure will conclude with the removal of both spermatic cords from the inguinal canals.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to take the lumbar and gonadal vessels, leaving two lumbar veins clipped intentionally. A small lymph node is visible, which the surgeon plans to remove. The surgical field includes the aorta, with ongoing suction to clear the area.
The surgeon identifies the adrenal vein and positions a clip while holding the renal pelvis and ureter in view. A scissor is used to assist in the procedure.

The surgeon requests a clip and scissor while ensuring the ureters are intact. The surgical team prepares to remove the cord, with adjustments to the robotic arms for better access. The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument in the left hand, considering switching the scissor to the fourth arm for improved maneuverability.

The surgeon switches the scissors to the fourth arm for better access and safely retracts the instrument. The right spermatic cord is removed from the inguinal canal, and the surgeon prepares to remove the left cord, navigating around the sigmoid colon. The completed lymph node dissections include paracaval, intertacaval, retrocaval, retroaortic, and paraaortic nodes, with visible nerve-sparing bundles.

The surgeon prepares to finish the dissection of the cord, utilizing the fourth robotic arm for better access. FloSeal is applied in the upper abdomen to promote clot formation and reduce lymphatic fluid leakage. The gonadal vein is dissected, taking care to avoid the ureter as the gonadal vein crosses anterior to it. The gonadal vein moves laterally while the ureter remains medial.
The surgeon adjusts the camera to focus on the dissection site from the opposite side, preparing to manipulate the cord.

The surgeon navigates the left hand to continue the dissection, encountering the vas and a vasal vessel that is prone to bleeding. The epigastric artery is visible, and the surgeon requests deeper access to this area. Sutures are noted, indicating a previous orchiectomy performed by another surgeon. The surgeon prepares to use an extraction sack for specimen removal.
